en.extract:
The inland thornbill (Acanthiza apicalis) was originally described by English ornithologist John Gould in The Birds of Australia.  Inland thornbills are within the order passerines. The inland thornbill belongs to the genus Acanthiza, which now has three more species than the eleven outlined by Gould in The Birds of Australia. The Noongar people of southwestern Western Australia call A. apicalis "Djoobi-Djoolbang". The inland thornbill is also known as the broad-tail thornbill and presently contains several subspecies that were once considered independent species. The word apicalis comes from the Latin for 'tipped'.


Taxonomy and evolution
Early classification of the Acanthiza, or thornbill genus, was inconsistent with the currently accepted taxonomy.  Historically, researchers recognised 10 -17 species in 1 – 3 genera. Separate traditional species of Thornbill have now been more accurately classified as subspecies than discrete species; however, the evolutionary relationships between inland thornbills and other Acanthizae are complicated. For example, the relationship between the inland thornbill and the brown thornbill (A. pusilla) and the mountain thornbill (A. katherina) is more complex than just classifying the genus. To further complicate the evolutionary relationships between species in the genus Acanthiza, two species exist outside of Australia in New Guinea. The New Guinea thornbill (A. murina) is found in the highlands of New Guinea, and it is not definitive how this species fits in with the classification of other thornbills. Still, some research has found the New Guinea thornbill to be closely related to A.nana or the yellow thornbill. Traditionally Acanthiza classification was conducted using morphology and plumage patterns.
Recent research using base-substitution data from nuclear DNA (nDNA) sequences sampled from Acanthiza species identified five separate species groups. Inland thornbills are in the same species group as A, pusilla, A. ewingii, and A. katherina. The other species groups are one containing A. inornate, A. uropygialis, A, reguloides, and A. iredalei, one containing A. nana and A. lineata, one containing A. chrysorrhoa, and one for A. robustirostris. The results of the Acanthizae nDNA samples were compared to samples from the gerygone, whiteface and weebill genera, as these species have previously been found to be in the same tribe as Acanthiza.


Subspecies
There are four recognised subspecies of inland thornbill with different geographic regions:

Acanthiza apicalis apicalis: the nominate subspecies.  Southwestern Western Australia
Acanthiza apicalis albiventris: Eastern South Australia and northwestern Victoria; central New South Wales and Queensland.
Acanthiza apicalis cinerascens: West-central Queensland
Acanthiza apicalis whitlocki: Inland southern Australia, extending to the coast in the west.


Description
The inland thornbill is very similar to the brown thornbill (A. pusilla) in appearance, and these two species are sometimes considered conspecific. The brown thornbill is slightly smaller than the inland thornbill at approximately 10 cm (3.9 in) compared to 11.5 cm (4.5 in). The inland thornbill has white speckling on the forehead, while the brown thornbill has rufous speckling and is less grey than the inland thornbill. Range and habitat is the easiest way to differentiate these two species. Where both species occur in eastern Australia, the inland thornbill can be found in drier habitats than the brown thornbill; however, in southwestern Western Australia (outside of the brown thornbill's range) inland thornbill can be found in wetter forests.
Gould originally described the inland thornbill as having a larger and rounder tail, a broad and distinct band of black tipped with white across the ends of the tail feathers, and a larger overall size than other Acanthiza species. The inland thornbill has several calls similar to the brown thornbill and a high-pitched tsee-tsee. Inland thornbills have also been found to be proficient in mimicking the calls of other birds, such as the pied butcherbird (Cracticus nigrogularis), rufous whistler (Pachycephala rufiventris), bush stone-curlew (Burhinus grallarius), willie wagtail (Rhipidura leucophrys), grey fantail (Rhipidura albiscapa), and various cuckoos.
Inland thornbills are long-lived, with one instance of the same bird being retrapped and banded over seven years after the first occasion. A typical lifespan for an inland thornbill is 5.7 years.


Ecology


Habitat and distribution
As the distribution range of the inland thornbills is wide, inland thornbills inhabit a variety of different habitat types. Inland thornbills inhabit arid woodlands and scrubs throughout Australia, including the mulga (Acacia aneura) of the Mallee and the Gibson Desert. Contrary to the common name, inland thornbills can also be found in mangrove forests in southwestern Western Australia and dense forests and coastal heaths. While the distribution range of inland thornbills is large, they are not a migratory species.


Feeding
Typically, inland thornbills do not feed on the ground but instead in foliage as shrubs-canopy or generalised feeders. Inland thornbills are known to forage in the foliage of trees and the dense understory of shrubs in small parties or pairs but have been known to feed with other small birds in a mixed flock. The typical diet of inland thornbills consists of spiders and other small insects.


Breeding
The genus Acanthiza is native to New Guinea and Australia and comprises 12 sexually monomorphic species. The inland thornbill male is larger than the female. Some species of Acanthiza breed in pairs, while others are cooperative breeders. Inland thornbills are believed to breed in pairs as they are typically found alone or in pairs; this behaviour is similar to the known pair breeder, the brown thornbill. However, the ancestral breeding state for the genus Acanthiza and the tribe Acanthizini is cooperative breeding. Within the Acanthiza, on two separate occasions, pair breeding is believed to have evolved, but this is not believed to be due to environmental variables.
The nest of the inland thornbill is domed and is near the ground, nestled in a shrub. The nest is constructed of dry grass and bark fragments bound by cobwebs; near the top is a side entrance, and the nest is lined with feathers and soft grass. Inland thornbill breeding season is July to December with three white eggs with reddish freckles and blotches.


Predators
Members of the genus Acanthiza and family Maluridae (Australasian wrens including the superb fairywren Malurus cyaneus) often foster the shining bronze-cuckoo (Chrysococcyx lucidus). The inland thornbill is also known to be parasitised by fan-tailed cuckoos (Cuculus pyrrhophanus). Inland thornbills are also susceptible to cat predation.


Conservation
The IUCN Red List assessed inland thornbills as Least Concern on 01 October 2016. The justification was that the inland thornbill is not limited to a niche habitat and has a distribution range above the Vulnerable listing threshold. It is acknowledged that while the population trend of inland thornbills is decreasing, it is not rapidly approaching the >30% decline over three generations or ten years that would qualify Vulnerable due to population trend. Furthermore, while the exact population size of the inland thornbill is not quantified, it is believed to be greater than the threshold for Vulnerable status under the population size criterion.
Under the Nature Conservation Act 1992 in Queensland, the conservation status of the inland thornbill is Least Concern. Furthermore, the conservation status is secure in all states and territories where the inland thornbill can be found.


Threats and human interaction


Habitat destruction
A definitive relationship between inland thornbills and habitat destruction is not clear. Some research suggests that inland thornbills have historically increased abundance when a jarrah wood forest is logged, but habitat trees are left after the logged area was burned. In comparison, other studies have found that inland thornbills struggle to recover after a wildfire or drought. One possible explanation for inhibited regeneration of the abundance of inland thornbills is poor powers of recolonisation. It is also possible that inland thornbills require less frequent fire events.


Poetry
Poet John Kinsella wrote a poem for the British magazine the New Statesman, titled "Inland Thornbills".


References


External links
 Media related to Acanthiza apicalis at Wikimedia Commons
 Data related to Acanthiza apicalis at Wikispecies

The '''inland thornbill''' ('''''Acanthiza apicalis''''') was originally described by English ornithologist [[John Gould]] in ''[[The Birds of Australia (Gould)|The Birds of Australia]].''<ref name=":0">{{Cite book |last1=Gould |first1=Elizabeth |url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/bibliography/105698 |title=The birds of Australia |last2=Gould |first2=John |last3=Richter |first3=Henry Constantine |date=1840 |publisher=Printed by R. and J. E. Taylor; pub. by the author |location=London |doi=10.5962/bhl.title.105698}}</ref>  Inland thornbills are within the order [[Passerine|passerines]].<ref name=":10">{{Cite book |last=Schodde |first=Richard |title=The directory of Australian birds. Volume 1, Passerines: a taxonomic and zoogeographic atlas of the biodiversity of birds in Australia and its territories |date=1999 |publisher=CSIRO Pub |others=Ian J. Mason |isbn=978-0-643-10086-2 |location=Collingwood, VIC |oclc=704565413}}</ref> The inland thornbill belongs to the genus ''[[Acanthiza]],'' which now has three more species than the eleven outlined by Gould in The Birds of Australia.<ref name=":0" /> The ''[[Noongar]]'' people of southwestern Western Australia call ''A. apicalis'' "Djoobi-Djoolbang".<ref name=":5">{{Cite web |title=Inland Thornbill • ReWild Perth |url=https://rewildperth.com.au/resource/inland-thornbill/ |access-date=2022-10-23 |website=ReWild Perth |language=en-AU}}</ref> The inland thornbill is also known as the broad-tail thornbill and presently contains several [[subspecies]] that were once considered independent species.<ref name=":1">{{Cite book |last=Cayley |first=Neville W. |title=What bird is that? |date=1987 |publisher=Angus & Robertson |others=Terence Lindsey |isbn=0-207-15556-9 |edition=Rev., expanded, deluxe |location=Sydney |oclc=156806692}}</ref> The word ''apicalis'' comes from the Latin for 'tipped'.<ref name=":1" />{{Short description|Species of bird}}
[[File:Bird illustration by Elizabeth Gould for Birds of Australia, digitally enhanced from rawpixel's own facsimile book197.jpg|thumb|Acanthiza apicalis plate from Birds of Australia (Gould)<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Gould |first=John |date=1848 |title=Acanthiza apicalis, Gould., Western Acanthiza, [Pl. 57] |journal=The Birds of Australia |volume=3 |doi=10.5962/p.313088|doi-access=free }}</ref>]]

== Taxonomy and evolution ==
Early classification of the ''Acanthiza'', or thornbill genus, was inconsistent with the currently accepted taxonomy.  Historically, researchers recognised 10 -17 species in 1 – 3 genera.<ref name=":2">{{Cite journal |last=Nicholls |first=James A. |date=2001-03-01 |title=Molecular systematics of the thornbills, Acanthiza |journal=Emu - Austral Ornithology |volume=101 |issue=1 |pages=33–37 |doi=10.1071/MU00064 |bibcode=2001EmuAO.101...33N |s2cid=82927731 |issn=0158-4197}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Mathews |first=Gregory M. |title=A manual of the birds of Australia |date=1921 |publisher=H.F. & G. Witherby |oclc=1049634876}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Mayr |first1=Ernst |last2=Serventy |first2=D. L. |date=1938-11-01 |title=A Review of the Genus Acanthiza Vigors and Horsfield |journal=Emu - Austral Ornithology |volume=38 |issue=3 |pages=245–292 |doi=10.1071/MU938245 |bibcode=1938EmuAO..38..245M |issn=0158-4197}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last=Mack |first=G. |date=1936-11-01 |title=A systematic revision of the Australian thornbills |url=https://museumsvictoria.com.au/collections-research/journals/memoirs-of-museum-victoria/volume-10-1936/86-118/ |journal=Memoirs of Museum Victoria |language=English |volume=10 |pages=86–118 |doi=10.24199/j.mmv.1936.10.04|doi-access=free }}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last=Campbell |first=A.G. |date=1923-07-01 |title=Synopsis of Species of Thornbills |journal=Emu - Austral Ornithology |volume=23 |issue=1 |pages=28–30 |doi=10.1071/MU923028 |bibcode=1923EmuAO..23...28C |issn=0158-4197|doi-access=free }}</ref> Separate traditional species of Thornbill have now been more accurately classified as subspecies than discrete species; however, the evolutionary relationships between inland thornbills and other ''Acanthizae'' are complicated. For example, the relationship between the inland thornbill and the [[brown thornbill]] (''A. pusilla)'' and the [[mountain thornbill]] (''A. katherina)'' is more complex than just classifying the genus.<ref name=":2" /> To further complicate the evolutionary relationships between species in the genus ''Acanthiza,'' two species exist outside of Australia in [[New Guinea]].<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Mellor |first=J W |date=1919 |title=Description of a new species of Climacteris |journal=South Australian Ornithologist |volume=4 |pages=5–6 |doi=10.5962/bhl.part.29854 |issn=0038-2973}}</ref> The [[New Guinea thornbill]] (''A. murina'') is found in the highlands of New Guinea, and it is not definitive how this species fits in with the classification of other thornbills. Still, some research has found the New Guinea thornbill to be closely related to ''A.nana'' or the [[yellow thornbill]].<ref name=":3">{{Cite journal |last1=Nicholls |first1=James A. |last2=Double |first2=Michael C. |last3=Rowell |first3=David M. |last4=Magrath |first4=Robert D. |date=2000 |title=The Evolution of Cooperative and Pair Breeding in Thornbills Acanthiza (Pardalotidae) |journal=Journal of Avian Biology |volume=31 |issue=2 |pages=165–176 |doi=10.1034/j.1600-048X.2000.310208.x |jstor=3676990 |issn=0908-8857}}</ref> Traditionally ''Acanthiza'' classification was conducted using [[Morphology (biology)|morphology]] and plumage patterns.<ref name=":2" />

Recent research using base-substitution data from [[nuclear DNA]] (nDNA) sequences sampled from ''Acanthiza'' species identified five separate species groups.<ref name=":2" /> Inland thornbills are in the same species group as ''A, pusilla,'' ''A. ewingii,'' and ''A. katherina.'' The other species groups are one containing ''A. inornate, A. uropygialis, A, reguloides, and A. iredalei'', one containing ''A. nana'' and ''A. lineata,'' one containing ''A. chrysorrhoa,'' and one for ''A. robustirostris.''<ref name=":2" /> The results of the ''Acanthizae'' nDNA samples were compared to samples from the [[gerygone]], whiteface and [[weebill]] genera, as these species have previously been found to be in the same [[Tribe (biology)|tribe]] as ''Acanthiza''.<ref>{{Cite book |last1=Sibley |first1=Charles G. |title=Phylogeny and Classification of the Birds |last2=Ahlquist |first2=Jon E. |date=1991-01-23 |publisher=Yale University Press |isbn=978-0-300-23785-6 |language=en-GB |doi=10.2307/j.ctt1xp3v3r}}</ref>

===  Subspecies ===
There are four recognised subspecies of inland thornbill with different geographic regions:<ref>{{Cite web |title=Acanthiza apicalis (Broad-tailed Thornbill) - Avibase |url=https://avibase.bsc-eoc.org/species.jsp?avibaseid=4413633D |access-date=2022-10-23 |website=avibase.bsc-eoc.org}}</ref>

* ''Acanthiza apicalis apicalis'': the nominate subspecies.  Southwestern Western Australia
* ''Acanthiza apicalis albiventris:'' Eastern South Australia and northwestern Victoria; central New South Wales and Queensland.
* ''Acanthiza apicalis cinerascens'': West-central Queensland
* ''Acanthiza apicalis whitlocki'': Inland southern Australia, extending to the coast in the west.

[[File:Inland thornbill 0A2A2135.jpg|thumb|left|Inland thornbill]]
[[File:Brown Thornbill.jpg|thumb|left|Brown thornbill]]

== Description ==
The inland thornbill is very similar to the [[brown thornbill]] ''(A. pusilla)'' in appearance, and these two species are sometimes considered [[conspecific]].<ref name=":1" /> The brown thornbill is slightly smaller than the inland thornbill at approximately {{cvt|10|cm|in|abbr=on}} compared to {{cvt|11.5|cm|in|abbr=on}}.<ref name=":4">{{Cite book |last=Campbell |first=Iain |title=Birds of australia: a photographic guide |date=2015 |publisher=Princeton University Press |isbn=978-0-691-15727-6 |oclc=1285292977}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Schodde |first=Richard |title=The directory of Australian birds. Volume 1, Passerines: a taxonomic and zoogeographic atlas of the biodiversity of birds in Australia and its territories |date=1999 |publisher=CSIRO Pub |others=Ian J. Mason |isbn=978-0-643-10086-2 |location=Collingwood, VIC |oclc=704565413}}</ref> The inland thornbill has white speckling on the forehead, while the brown thornbill has rufous speckling and is less grey than the inland thornbill.<ref name=":4" /> Range and habitat is the easiest way to differentiate these two species. Where both species occur in eastern Australia, the inland thornbill can be found in drier habitats than the brown thornbill; however, in southwestern Western Australia (outside of the brown thornbill's range) inland thornbill can be found in wetter forests.<ref name=":4" />

Gould originally described the inland thornbill as having a larger and rounder tail, a broad and distinct band of black tipped with white across the ends of the tail feathers, and a larger overall size than other ''Acanthiza'' species.<ref name=":0" /> The inland thornbill has several calls similar to the brown thornbill and a high-pitched ''tsee-tsee''.<ref name=":1" /> Inland thornbills have also been found to be proficient in mimicking the calls of other birds, such as the [[pied butcherbird]] (''Cracticus nigrogularis''), [[rufous whistler]] (''Pachycephala rufiventris''), [[bush stone-curlew]] (''Burhinus grallarius''), [[willie wagtail]] (''Rhipidura leucophrys''), [[grey fantail]] (''Rhipidura albiscapa''), and various cuckoos.<ref name=":6">{{Cite web |title=Inland Thornbill {{!}} BirdLife Australia |url=https://www.birdlife.org.au/bird-profile/inland-thornbill |access-date=2022-10-23 |website=www.birdlife.org.au |language=en |archive-date=23 October 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221023130254/https://www.birdlife.org.au/bird-profile/inland-thornbill }}</ref>

Inland thornbills are long-lived, with one instance of the same bird being retrapped and banded over seven years after the first occasion.<ref name=":3" /> A typical lifespan for an inland thornbill is 5.7 years.<ref name=":7">{{cite iucn |author=BirdLife International. |year=2024 |title=''Acanthiza apicalis'' |volume=2024 |article-number=e.T22731025A253980766 |doi=10.2305/IUCN.UK.2024-2.RLTS.T22731025A253980766.en |access-date=12 February 2025}}</ref>

== Ecology ==

=== Habitat and distribution ===
As the distribution range of the inland thornbills is wide, inland thornbills inhabit a variety of different habitat types. Inland thornbills inhabit arid woodlands and scrubs throughout Australia, including the mulga (''[[Acacia aneura]]'') of the [[Mallee (habit)|Mallee]] and the [[Gibson Desert]].<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Bell |first1=David T. |last2=Bell |first2=Roma C. |last3=Loneragan |first3=William A. |date=2016 |title=Winter avifauna of Wanjarri Nature Reserve and Lake Mason Conservation Park of the Eastern Goldfields, Western Australia |url=https://www.birdlife.org.au/afo/index.php/afo/article/view/2053 |journal=Australian Field Ornithology |language=en-US |volume=33 |pages=198–205 |doi=10.20938/afo33198205|s2cid=89265686 |url-access=subscription }}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Burbidge |first1=A. A. |last2=Fuller |first2=P. J. |date=2007-06-01 |title=Gibson Desert birds: responses to drought and plenty |journal=Emu - Austral Ornithology |volume=107 |issue=2 |pages=126–134 |doi=10.1071/MU06044 |bibcode=2007EmuAO.107..126B |s2cid=84571245 |issn=0158-4197}}</ref> Contrary to the common name, inland thornbills can also be found in [[Mangrove forest|mangrove forests]] in southwestern Western Australia and dense forests and coastal heaths.<ref name=":1" /> While the distribution range of inland thornbills is large, they are not a [[migratory species]].<ref name=":7" />
[[File:Acacia aneura habit.jpg|thumb|right|Mulga {Acacia aneura)]]

=== Feeding ===
Typically, inland thornbills do not feed on the ground but instead in foliage as shrubs-canopy or generalised feeders.<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":8">{{Cite journal |last1=Coman |first1=Amelia |last2=Potter |first2=Sally |last3=Moritz |first3=Craig |last4=Campbell |first4=Catriona D. |last5=Joseph |first5=Leo |date=November 2020 |title=Biotic and abiotic drivers of evolution in some Australian thornbills (Passeriformes: Acanthiza ) in allopatry, sympatry, and parapatry including a case of character displacement |journal=Journal of Zoological Systematics and Evolutionary Research |language=en |volume=58 |issue=4 |pages=1290–1302 |doi=10.1111/jzs.12355 |s2cid=213394343 |issn=0947-5745|doi-access=free }}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Leavesley |first1=Adam J. |last2=Cary |first2=Geoffrey J. |last3=Edwards |first3=Glenn P. |last4=Gill |first4=A. Malcolm |date=2010-11-05 |title=The effect of fire on birds of mulga woodland in arid central Australia |url=https://www.publish.csiro.au/wf/WF09028 |journal=International Journal of Wildland Fire |language=en |volume=19 |issue=7 |pages=949–960 |doi=10.1071/WF09028 |issn=1448-5516|url-access=subscription }}</ref> Inland thornbills are known to forage in the foliage of trees and the dense [[understory]] of shrubs in small parties or pairs but have been known to feed with other small birds in a mixed flock.<ref name=":6" /> The typical diet of inland thornbills consists of spiders and other small insects.<ref name=":6" />
[[File:Inland Thornbill - Wyperfeld NP - Victoria S4E4040 (22200831530).jpg|thumb|right|Inland thornbill, Wyperfeld National Park, Victoria]]

=== Breeding ===
The genus ''Acanthiza'' is native to New Guinea and Australia and comprises 12 [[sexually monomorphic]] species.<ref name=":8" /> The inland thornbill male is larger than the female.<ref name=":6" /> Some species of ''Acanthiza'' breed in pairs, while others are [[cooperative breeders]].<ref name=":8" /> Inland thornbills are believed to breed in pairs as they are typically found alone or in pairs; this behaviour is similar to the known pair breeder, the brown thornbill.<ref name=":3" /> However, the ancestral breeding state for the genus ''Acanthiza'' and the tribe [[Acanthizidae|Acanthizini]] is cooperative breeding. Within the ''Acanthiza,'' on two separate occasions, pair breeding is believed to have evolved, but this is not believed to be due to environmental variables.<ref name=":3" />

The nest of the inland thornbill is domed and is near the ground, nestled in a shrub.<ref name=":1" /> The nest is constructed of dry grass and bark fragments bound by cobwebs; near the top is a side entrance, and the nest is lined with feathers and soft grass.<ref name=":1" /> Inland thornbill breeding season is July to December with three white eggs with reddish freckles and blotches.<ref name=":1" />

[[File:Shining Bronze-Cuckoo Dayboro.JPG|thumb|right|Shining bronze-cuckoo]]
===Predators===
Members of the genus ''Acanthiza'' and family [[Maluridae]] (Australasian wrens including the [[superb fairywren]] ''Malurus cyaneus'') often foster the [[shining bronze-cuckoo]] (''Chrysococcyx lucidus'').<ref name=":0" /> The inland thornbill is also known to be parasitised by [[Fan-tailed cuckoo|fan-tailed cuckoos]] (''Cuculus pyrrhophanus'').''<ref name=":9">{{Cite journal |last1=Brooker |first1=M. G. |last2=Rowley |first2=I. |date=1991 |title=Impact of Wildfire on the Nesting behaviour of Birds in Heathland |url=https://www.publish.csiro.au/wr/wr9910249 |journal=Wildlife Research |language=en |volume=18 |issue=3 |pages=249–263 |doi=10.1071/wr9910249 |bibcode=1991WildR..18..249B |issn=1448-5494|url-access=subscription }}</ref>'' Inland thornbills are also susceptible to cat predation''.<ref name=":5" />''

== Conservation ==
The [[IUCN Red List]] assessed inland thornbills as [[Least Concern (IUCN Red List of Ecosystems)|Least Concern]] on 01 October 2016.<ref name=":7" /> The justification was that the inland thornbill is not limited to a niche habitat and has a distribution range above the [[Vulnerable species|Vulnerable]] listing threshold.<ref name=":7" /> It is acknowledged that while the population trend of inland thornbills is decreasing, it is not rapidly approaching the >30% decline over three generations or ten years that would qualify Vulnerable due to population trend.<ref name=":7" /> Furthermore, while the exact population size of the inland thornbill is not quantified, it is believed to be greater than the threshold for Vulnerable status under the population size criterion.<ref name=":7" />

Under the ''[[Nature Conservation Act 1992]]'' in Queensland, the conservation status of the inland thornbill is Least Concern.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2014-10-20 |title=Species profile {{!}} Environment, land and water |url=https://apps.des.qld.gov.au/species-search/details/?id=1418 |access-date=2022-10-23 |website=apps.des.qld.gov.au |language=en-AU}}</ref> Furthermore, the conservation status is secure in all states and territories where the inland thornbill can be found.<ref name=":6" />

== Threats and human interaction ==

=== Habitat destruction ===
A definitive relationship between inland thornbills and habitat destruction is not clear. Some research suggests that inland thornbills have historically increased abundance when a [[jarrah]] wood forest is logged, but habitat trees are left after the logged area was burned.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Abbott |first1=Ian |last2=Mellican |first2=Amanda |last3=Craig |first3=Michael D. |last4=Williams |first4=Matthew |last5=Liddelow |first5=Graeme |last6=Wheeler |first6=Ian |date=2003 |title=Short-term logging and burning impacts on species richness, abundance and community structure of birds in open eucalypt forest in Western Australia |url=http://www.publish.csiro.au/?paper=WR02058 |journal=Wildlife Research |language=en |volume=30 |issue=4 |page=321 |doi=10.1071/WR02058 |bibcode=2003WildR..30..321A |issn=1035-3712|url-access=subscription }}</ref> In comparison, other studies have found that inland thornbills struggle to recover after a wildfire or drought.<ref name=":9" /> One possible explanation for inhibited regeneration of the abundance of inland thornbills is poor powers of recolonisation.<ref name=":9" /> It is also possible that inland thornbills require less frequent fire events.<ref name=":9" />

=== Poetry ===
Poet [[John Kinsella (poet)|John Kinsella]] wrote a poem for the British magazine the ''[[New Statesman]]'', titled "Inland Thornbills".<ref>{{Cite web |last=Kinsella |first=John |date=2018-06-06 |title=The NS Poem: Inland Thornbills |url=https://www.newstatesman.com/culture/2018/06/ns-poem-inland-thornbills |access-date=2022-10-23 |website=New Statesman |language=en-US}}</ref>
